perturbation.original_text: FASC shall create and maintain all necessary books and records in accordance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, including but not limited to records required by Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and the rules thereunder, as the same may be amended from time to time, pertaining to the Services performed by it and not otherwise created and maintained by another party.
perturbation.changed_text: FASC shall endeavor to create and maintain books and records, potentially aligned with applicable laws, rules and regulations, including records related to Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and its rules, as amended. Duplication of records maintained by another party is discouraged.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation by changing 'shall create and maintain' to 'shall endeavor to create and maintain,' making the record-keeping duty less stringent. The phrase 'potentially aligned' introduces ambiguity regarding compliance with laws. Discouraging duplication, while seeming reasonable, could lead to incomplete records if the other party fails to maintain them properly, violating regulatory requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 mandates that investment companies and their advisors maintain specific records. The modified text, by using 'endeavor' and 'potentially aligned', creates ambiguity and weakens the mandatory nature of this record-keeping requirement. Further, discouraging duplication could lead to a failure to maintain complete records as required by the law, if the other party fails to maintain the proper records.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-30 - Accounts and records | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-30 - Accounts and records U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Maintenance of records (1) In general Each registered investment company , and each underwriter, broker, dealer, or investment adviser that is a majority-owned subsidiary of such a company, shall maintain and preserve such records (as defined in section 78c(a)(37) of this title ) for such period or periods as the Commission, by rules and regulations, may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. Each investment adviser that is not a majority-owned subsidiary of, and each depositor of any registered investment company, and each principal underwriter for any registered investment company other than a closed-end company, shall maintain and preserve for such period or periods as the Commission shall prescribe by rules and regulations, such records as are necessary or appropriate to record such person’ s transactions with such registered company. Each person having custody or use of the securities, deposits, or credits of a registered investment company shall maintain and preserve all records that relate to the custody or use by such person of the securities, deposits, or credits of the registered investment company for such period or periods as the Commission, by rule or regulation, may prescribe, as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (2) Minimizing compliance burden In exercising its authority under this subsection, the Commission shall take such steps as it deems necessary or appropriate, consistent with the public interest and for the protection of investors, to avoid unnecessary recordkeeping by, and minimize the compliance burden on, persons required to maintain records under this
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the legal obligation to maintain records as mandated by Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (15 USC 80a-30). The original text states "FASC shall create and maintain all necessary books and records," while the changed text says "FASC shall endeavor to create and maintain books and records, potentially aligned with applicable laws." Replacing "shall" with "shall endeavor" weakens the obligation. The phrase "potentially aligned" creates ambiguity. Furthermore, discouraging duplication could lead to incomplete records if the other party fails to maintain them properly. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that each registered investment company "shall maintain and preserve such records...as the Commission, by rules and regulations, may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors." The modified text undermines this mandatory requirement, creating a legal contradiction as it makes compliance discretionary and uncertain.

perturbation.original_text: The Adviser shall indemnify FASC and shall hold FASC harmless from and against any liability to any Account or to any other person which may incurred by or asserted against FASC for any action taken or omitted by it in performing the Services in accordance with the above standards, and any expenses (including the reasonable fees and expenses of its counsel) which may be incurred by FASC in investigating or defending itself against the assertion of any such liability.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser may indemnify FASC from liability to any Account or person incurred by FASC's actions or omissions while performing Services, subject to the Adviser's discretion, potentially covering FASC's counsel fees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall indemnify' to 'may indemnify' significantly weakens the indemnification obligation, making it discretionary. Adding 'subject to the Adviser's discretion' further reinforces this ambiguity. The original text clearly obligates the Adviser to indemnify FASC for liabilities arising from performing the services, while the modified version introduces uncertainty, potentially violating fiduciary duty requirements for investment advisers.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 establishes a fiduciary duty for investment advisers. An ambiguous indemnification clause can allow the adviser to avoid covering losses from actions related to the services they provide. This contradicts this section's mandate to act in the best interest of their clients, potentially favoring the firm’s interest over the client’s.

Prohibited transactions by investment advisers It shall be unlawful for any investment adviser by use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, directly or indirectly- (1) to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; (2) to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 - Prohibited transactions by investment advisers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 - Prohibited transactions by investment advisers U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next It shall be unlawful for any investment adviser by use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce , directly or indirectly— (1) to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; (2) to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which operates as a fraud or deceit upon any client or prospective client; (3) acting as principal for his own account, knowingly to sell any security to or purchase any security from a client, or acting as broker for a person other than such client, knowingly to effect any sale or purchase of any security for the account of such client, without disclosing to such client in writing before the completion of such transaction the capacity in which he is acting and obtaining the consent of the client to such transaction. The prohibitions of this paragraph shall not apply to any transaction with a customer of a broker or dealer if such broker or dealer is not acting as an investment adviser in relation to such transaction; or (4) to engage in any act, practice, or course of business which is fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative. The Commission shall, for the purposes of this paragraph (4) by rules and regulations define, and prescribe means reasonably designed to prevent, such acts, practices, and courses of business as are fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative. (Aug. 22, 1940, ch. 686, title II, § 206, 54 Stat. 852 ; Pub. L. 86–750 , §§ 8, 9, Sept. 13, 1960 , 74 Stat. 887 ; Pub. L. 111–203, title IX, § 985(e)(2) , July 21, 2010 , 124 Stat. 1935 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2010—Par. (3). Pub. L. 111–203 inserted “or” at end. 1960— Pub. L.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the mandatory 'shall indemnify' with the discretionary 'may indemnify,' significantly weakening the obligation of the Adviser to protect FASC from liabilities. This shift introduces ambiguity by adding 'subject to the Adviser's discretion.' According to the Law Explanation, this contradicts Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, which mandates investment advisers act in their clients' best interests. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6, prohibits investment advisers from engaging in 'any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client'. The weakening of the indemnification clause could be interpreted as the adviser potentially prioritizing their own financial interests over the client's protection from liabilities, which the original contract clearly intended to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may generally be interpreted under principles informed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania' to 'may generally be interpreted under principles informed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania,' the agreement no longer mandates that Pennsylvania law governs the agreement, but rather suggests it as a possible interpretive guide. This creates ambiguity as to which jurisdiction's laws will ultimately control the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles regarding Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct statutory violation, the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 (though it's a restatement, it reflects widely accepted common law principles) deals with choice of law in contracts. Changing the governing law clause to be permissive rather than mandatory introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to disputes over which jurisdiction's laws apply. Clear choice of law provisions are generally upheld to ensure predictability and enforceability of contracts, and ambiguity undermines this principle, creating a situation where a court might disregard the parties' intended choice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall be governed by' to 'may generally be interpreted under principles informed by,' undermining the original clear choice of law. The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, highlights the importance of clear choice of law provisions. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By making the governing law permissive, the changed text increases the likelihood of such a conflict, contradicting the principle of clear contractual obligations and potentially leading to enforceability issues.
